description: A thin object has little distance between opposing surfaces or little substance across its cross-section. Phi also uses 'lemi' for sparse material or a slender body. Width is separately 'hieru' (narrow); a narrow object need not be thin.
articulatory_notes: The tongue tip contacts the alveolar ridge for 'l' and releases into true-mid front 'e'. The lips then close for voiced nasal 'm' and reopen into high-front 'i'. Stress falls on the first syllable.
usage_notes: The phrase 'lemi moru' describes a thin wall, while 'lemi sima' describes thin thread or strands. The contrast with 'theru' (thick) concerns cross-section or material body rather than overall size.

semantic_domains.physical: little material thickness or sparse substance
semantic_domains.spatial: a small distance between opposing surfaces or across a cross-section
